---
title: "LaTeX Skills That Make Manuscripts Look Professional"
subtitle: "Academic writing workshop for statistics and data science"
author: "Jun Yan"
institute: "Department of Statistics, University of Connecticut"
date: ""
aspectratio: 169
bibliography: ../book.bib
csl: apa.csl
format:
  beamer:
    theme: boxes
    slide-level: 2
    navigation: horizontal
    header-includes: |
      \setbeamertemplate{navigation symbols}{}
      \setbeamertemplate{footline}[page number]
      \usefonttheme[onlymath]{serif}
      \usepackage{amsmath, amssymb, booktabs}
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Starting Point: What Looks Unprofessional?

We will start from symptoms, not rules.

The goal is to notice the small LaTeX decisions that make readers spend more
energy than necessary.

Reader energy matters: good scientific writing manages reader expectations and
reduces avoidable cognitive work [@gopen1990science; @gopen2004expectations].

## Plan for the Hour

| Time | Activity |
|---:|---|
| 0--10 | Top annoyance audit |
| 10--25 | Repair the highest-impact LaTeX problems |
| 25--40 | Spot problems in a real PDF |
| 40--52 | Tables, figures, citations, and references |
| 52--60 | Personal checklist and Q&A |

## Top Annoyances to Avoid

From the JDS author workshop:

1. no punctuation for equations
2. raster graphs for line art
3. negative signs not in math mode
4. wrong math symbols and operators
5. inconsistent notation
6. confusing `\citet` and `\citep`

## Top Annoyances to Avoid

7. unordered or redundant keywords
8. nonuse of cross-referencing
9. unclean BibTeX source
10. no line numbers for reviewing
11. no supplement for reproducibility checking

Most are easy to fix. The hard part is learning to see them.

## Quick Poll

Which problems do you notice most often?

- equations that do not read as sentences
- blurry or distorted figures
- spreadsheet-looking tables
- inconsistent symbols
- broken citations or references
- source files nobody can review

## A Professional Manuscript Has Two Layers

Visible layer:

- equations, tables, figures, references
- spacing, alignment, captions, numbering

Source layer:

- clean labels
- reproducible figures and tables
- readable `.tex`
- controlled bibliography data

## Compile and Read the Warnings

Use a tool that runs the needed LaTeX passes:

```bash
latexmk -pdf manuscript
```

Then read the log for:

- undefined citations
- undefined or duplicated labels
- overfull boxes
- missing files

## Equation Problem: Missing Punctuation

Bad:

```tex
The estimator is
\[
\hat\theta = \arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta)
\]
It is consistent.
```

Result:

The estimator is
$$
\hat\theta = \arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta)
$$

It is consistent.

What to notice:

- the equation has no punctuation
- the next sentence feels disconnected
- the display interrupts the prose

## Equation Repair: Read It as Prose

Better:

```tex
The estimator is
\[
  \hat\theta = \arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta),
\]
and it is consistent under the regularity conditions.
```

Result:

The estimator is
$$
  \hat\theta = \arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta),
$$

and it is consistent under the regularity conditions.

## Inline Math: Keep It Light

Bad:

```tex
The statistic $\frac{\bar X - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt n}$ is compared with ...
```

Result: The statistic $\frac{\bar X - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt n}$ is compared with ...

Better:

```tex
The statistic $(\bar X - \mu_0)/(S/\sqrt n)$ is compared with ...
```

Result: The statistic $(\bar X - \mu_0)/(S/\sqrt n)$ is compared with ...

Use display math when the expression carries real weight.

## Use Math Operators

Bad:

```tex
$log(x)$, $exp(x)$, $min_i x_i$, $P(A)$
```

Result: $log(x)$, $exp(x)$, $min_i x_i$, $P(A)$

Better:

```tex
$\log(x)$, $\exp(x)$, $\min_i x_i$, $\Pr(A)$
```

Result: $\log(x)$, $\exp(x)$, $\min_i x_i$, $\Pr(A)$

Define special operators once when needed:

```tex
\DeclareMathOperator*{\argmax}{arg\,max}
```

## Consistency Is a Statistical Virtue

Common signals of carelessness:

- `n` versus `$n$`
- `AIC` versus `$\mathrm{AIC}$`
- `MSE` versus `$\mathrm{MSE}$`
- `$\theta_{LS}$` versus `$\theta_{\mathrm{LS}}$`

Notation should be boringly consistent.

## Mini Repair

Find and fix the LaTeX problems:

```tex
Since
\[
MSE(\hat\theta)=E(\hat\theta-\theta)^2
\]
\hat\theta is good when n is large.
```

Focus on punctuation, operators, notation, and sentence flow.

## One Possible Repair

```tex
The mean squared error of $\hat\theta$ is
\[
  \operatorname{MSE}(\hat\theta)
    = \operatorname{E}\{(\hat\theta - \theta)^2\}.
\]
This quantity is small when the sample size $n$ is large.
```

The exact notation can vary. The convention should not.

## Tables Should Reveal Comparisons

Good tables are designed, not dumped.

Use tables to make comparisons easy:

- align comparable numbers
- round to meaningful precision
- include units in headers or captions
- use consistent decimal places
- avoid vertical lines
- avoid shrinking a bad table

Clear presentation of numerical results is part of statistical writing
[@miller2015chicago; @morris2019using].

## Table Problem: Spreadsheet Habits

Bad:

```tex
\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
Method & Bias & RMSE \\ \hline
A & -0.02 & 0.31 \\ \hline
B & 0.01 & 0.28 \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
```

Result:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
Method & Bias & RMSE \\ \hline
A & -0.02 & 0.31 \\ \hline
B & 0.01 & 0.28 \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Notice the grid, repeated rules, and text hyphen for the negative number.

## Table Repair: `booktabs`

Better: no vertical lines, real rules, math minus signs.

```tex
\begin{tabular}{lrr}
\toprule
Method & Bias & RMSE \\
\midrule
A & $-0.02$ & 0.31 \\
B & 0.01 & 0.28 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
```

Result:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lrr}
\toprule
Method & Bias & RMSE \\
\midrule
A & $-0.02$ & 0.31 \\
B & 0.01 & 0.28 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

## Figures: Prefer Vector Graphics

For line art, save figures as vector graphics:

- `.pdf`
- `.eps`
- `.svg`, when supported

Avoid screenshots and low-resolution `.png` or `.jpg` files for statistical
plots.

Vector figures remain sharp when zoomed or printed.

## Figure Accessibility

Do not rely on color alone.

Use:

- colorblind-friendly palettes
- line types plus colors
- point shapes plus colors
- readable labels
- correct aspect ratios
- informative captions

## Captions and Floats

Use floats as layout tools:

```tex
\begin{figure}[tbp]
\centering
\includegraphics[width = 0.8\textwidth]{figures/rmse-by-n.pdf}
\caption{Root mean squared error across sample sizes.}
\label{fig:rmse}
\end{figure}
```

Avoid forcing everything with `[h]`.

## Citations Must Read Grammatically

Parenthetical:

```tex
The bootstrap is widely used for uncertainty quantification
\citep{efron1979bootstrap}.
```

Result: The bootstrap is widely used for uncertainty quantification (Efron,
1979).

Textual:

```tex
\citet{efron1979bootstrap} introduced the bootstrap as ...
```

Result: Efron (1979) introduced the bootstrap as ...

The citation command should fit the sentence.

## Cross-Reference Everything

Use labels:

```tex
\label{sec:methods}
\label{eq:score}
\label{tab:simulation}
\label{fig:rmse}
```

Use nonbreaking spaces:

```tex
Table~\ref{tab:simulation}
Figure~\ref{fig:rmse}
Section~\ref{sec:methods}
```

## Keep BibTeX Clean

Downloaded BibTeX is only a draft.

Check:

- author names
- capitalization
- journal names
- volume, issue, pages, DOI
- duplicate entries
- preprints that are now published

Protect capitalization: `{B}ayesian`, `{M}arkov Chain {M}onte {C}arlo`.

## Quarto and CSL

For Quarto/R Markdown manuscripts, citation formatting can be controlled with a
CSL file:

```yaml
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
```

This slide folder includes `apa.csl`.

Many statistics journals still expect BibTeX plus `natbib`.

## PDF Spotting Activity

Open an arXiv manuscript or a published paper.

Spend 5 minutes marking:

- one equation problem
- one table or figure problem
- one citation or reference problem
- one place where cross-referencing could help
- one thing the authors did well

Then we compare notes.

## Spotting Guide

Look for:

- equations without punctuation
- symbols that change fonts
- rasterized or distorted plots
- unexplained table entries
- hard-coded numbers instead of references
- broken line breaks before reference numbers
- bibliography entries with inconsistent capitalization

## Repair Clinic

Mark every issue:

```tex
From Figure \ref{fig1}, MSE is small when n increases.
\[
MSE(\hat\theta)=E(\hat\theta-\theta)^2
\]
See (Smith, 2020). The estimate is -0.04.

\begin{figure}[h]
\includegraphics[width=6in,height=2in]{plot.png}
\caption{plot}
\label{fig1}
\end{figure}
```

## One Cleaner Version

```tex
Figure~\ref{fig:mse} shows that the mean squared error decreases
as the sample size $n$ increases. Following \citet{smith2020},
we define
\[
  \operatorname{MSE}(\hat\theta)
    = \operatorname{E}\{(\hat\theta - \theta)^2\}.
\]
The estimate is $-0.04$.
```

Then use a vector figure file, `[tbp]`, and an informative caption.

## Pre-Submission Checklist

Before circulating a draft:

- compile from a clean checkout
- read the warnings
- inspect every display equation for punctuation
- zoom into every figure
- scan every table for alignment and precision
- check citations and BibTeX
- include line numbers if allowed
- prepare reproducibility materials

## What to Remember

Professional LaTeX is mostly habit:

- equations are prose
- notation is stable
- tables and figures serve comparisons
- citations are grammatical
- labels replace hard-coded numbers
- logs are read, not ignored

For details, use the writing notes as the reference.

## Selected References

::: {#refs}
:::
